title: Obione coriacea
taxonomy: Obione coriacea is classified within the family Amaranthaceae. This classification is based on its botanical characteristics, such as its leaf shape and flower structure. More specifically, Obione coriacea is a member of the genus Obione, which is a group of flowering plants native to the Americas. The genus Obione is often placed within the subfamily Amaranthoideae. Further classification may be possible at a more specific taxonomic level.
uses: Obione coriacea is a plant species with some reported uses in traditional medicine. Obione coriacea has been used in South America for its potential anti-inflammatory properties and as a treatment for various gastrointestinal issues. The plant has been used to help manage symptoms associated with digestive problems, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers. In addition, some traditional healers have employed Obione coriacea to treat respiratory issues like bronchitis. However, the efficacy and safety of these uses have not been extensively scientifically validated.

intro: Obione coriacea, also known as Beach Naupaka, is a coastal plant species native to Hawaii and other Pacific Island regions, characterized by its fragrant flowers and leathery leaves. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in wound healing, reducing inflammation, and protecting against oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Obione coriacea include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal preparations, such as teas, tinctures, and poultices, which are used to treat skin conditions, wounds, and respiratory issues, as well as to promote relaxation and reduce stress.
